title: Carolingian capitals, the majuscules for the Carolingian minuscule hand
subtitle: The capital letterforms that pair with Caroline minuscule in manuscript practice
excerpt: Carolingian minuscule used Roman rustica capitals as its companion majuscules in ninth-century manuscripts. The capital letterforms are upright, broad, and written with a broad-edge nib at a low angle. Thirteen stroke sequences cover the full alphabet.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), public domain, Internet Archive. Johnston reconstructs the historical capital hands that accompany minuscule scripts; his analysis of the Roman rustica stroke order informs this tutorial.

glossaryTerms.definition: A compressed, slightly slanted Roman capital hand used in manuscripts from the first to the ninth century. Written with a broad-edge nib held nearly flat to the writing line, producing narrow verticals and broad horizontals.
A capital letter, or a script composed entirely of capital-size letters. In mixed-register manuscripts, the majuscule is the form used at the start of sentences and proper nouns.

content.text: form. Later, scribes adopted square Roman capitals instead. The hand was reformed under Charlemagne's court in the late eighth century. The capitals here follow the rustica style of Caroline Gospel books. They are upright and broad-bodied. The nib is held at 5-10° from the horizontal: far flatter than the 30° Foundational angle.

The flat pen angle produces narrow verticals and wide horizontals: the reverse of Foundational's proportions. The height is 7-8 nib-widths for the capital body; no ascenders or descenders in pure capital work.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, size 3 (slug: nib-mitchell-roundhand-3)
items.description: The mid-range Mitchell Roundhand nib. At 7-8 nib-widths x-height the capital body is approximately 9-10 mm tall: a comfortable working size.

items.name: Straight pen holder (slug: straight-pen-holder)
items.description: These capitals are written with a straight holder; the flat pen angle is achieved by rotating the hand and arm rather than canting the holder.

items.name: Iron-gall ink (slug: iron-gall-ink)
items.description: Period-appropriate. Modern iron-gall in a non-corrosive formulation.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm (slug: cartridge-paper-220)
items.description: Ruled with guidelines at 7-8 nib-widths for the capital height.

content.text: Hold the pen at approximately 5-10° from horizontal: the nib edge is nearly parallel to the writing line, the corner barely lifted. The pen sits between the index and middle finger, supported by the thumb, with the hand resting on the outer edge of the little finger. At this angle, a horizontal stroke is the broadest mark the nib produces; a vertical stroke is the thinnest. This is the fundamental characteristic that distinguishes rustica from Foundational (30° angle) and Italic (45° angle).

A: two diagonal strokes meeting at the apex (strokes 1 and 2), then a horizontal crossbar (stroke 3). The diagonals are thin because they run at approximately 45° to the pen; the crossbar is broad. Start each diagonal at the apex and pull downward.
B: a vertical stem (stroke 1), two bumps on the right (strokes 2 and 3). The upper bump is smaller than the lower. Both bumps are broad because the curved strokes cross the pen at a wide angle.

C: a single curved stroke opened slightly at top and bottom. Begin at 2 o'clock, curve to the left, finish at 4 o'clock. The maximum width of the curve is broader than the stem of A because the curve crosses the pen angle.

E: vertical stem (stroke 1), three horizontal arms (strokes 2, 3, 4: top, middle, bottom). All three arms are broad. The middle arm is slightly shorter than the top and bottom. The bottom arm carries a slight foot serif formed by a second stroke pulled inward.

H: two vertical stems (strokes 1 and 2), connected by a broad horizontal crossbar (stroke 3) positioned at the optical mid-height, slightly above the arithmetic centre. N: vertical (stroke 1), diagonal (stroke 2) from top left to bottom right, vertical (stroke 3). The diagonal is thin; the verticals have hairline serifs. O: formed in two curved strokes (the left-opening C shape, then the right). The letter is slightly narrower than a circle. U: mirrored: left vertical with a base curve joining a right vertical. V: two diagonal strokes, meeting at the foot. W: four diagonal strokes.

Practise each letter 20 times at a consistent 7-8 nib-widths height before moving to words. The most common error is letting the pen angle creep upward toward 30°, which makes the verticals too thick and removes the rustica character.
